nativeRange.habitat: Sandy areas of shallow lagoon reefs and coastal bays on silty slopes; reported from 5–88 m, typically 5–20 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Sand and rubble flats adjacent to reefs, usually as monogamous pairs sharing a burrow.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty foods: mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, blackworms, and quality prepared carnivore foods. As an obligate sand-sifter it spends much of its day picking through substrate for micro-invertebrates and detritus; established live sand and an attached refugium are commonly advised to support frequent small meals.

avoidWith: boisterous tankmates that outcompete it for food

summary: A pale-bodied sleeper goby marked with three dark brown bars and a black eyespot on the first dorsal fin. Generally peaceful toward other species but territorial against its own kind unless kept as a bonded pair; needs a deep fine sand bed for sifting.
careNotes: The Tiger Watchman Goby is a sand-sifting sleeper goby that constantly mouths and screens substrate for micro-fauna, helping keep the sand bed clean but also depleting it of microfauna over time. A deep (5+ cm) fine sand bed and an established/refugium-supported tank are generally advised, and frequent small feedings are recommended because individuals can lose condition if outcompeted at feeding time. Tankmates should be peaceful; conspecifics and other sleeper gobies tend to be fought unless introduced as a known male-female pair. A tightly fitting lid is strongly recommended because Valenciennea species have been known to jump when startled or harassed. Sources report a maximum size of roughly 12–15 cm (LiveAquaria gives ~12.7 cm / 5 in; FishBase reports 15 cm SL); the tank recommendation has been biased larger than LiveAquaria's 30-gallon figure to allow long-term housing, swimming space, and adequate sand-bed footprint.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity on rare occasions when a bonded pair is established; eggs are typically laid in the pair's burrow. Larval rearing is considered very difficult and is not routinely accomplished by hobbyists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet: 5 in max, 30 gal min, easy care level, peaceful but territorial with conspecifics, reef safe, carnivore, pH 8.1–8.4, 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, range, and habitat: max 15 cm SL, depth 5–88 m (typ. 5–20 m), preferred temperature 22–27°C, Indo-Pacific (Indian Ocean to western tropical Pacific), sandy areas of shallow lagoon reefs, lives in pairs. IUCN Least Concern. Tank minimum biased larger than LiveAquaria's 30 gal figure (rounded to 150 L) per the husbandry-caution rule given the species' need for swimming space and sand-bed footprint.

fish.breedingNotes: Bonded pairs have been reported to spawn in their shared burrow; larvae are very difficult to rear and captive-bred individuals are uncommon in the trade.
